record.title: Justine Eisenberg | Interview 1 | September 5, 2015
record.subject: Community History
Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front

point.title: Introduction
point.keywords: 1910;1912;1914;Explorers Club;family background;family traditions;Jewish family;Krakow, Poland;Manhattan, New York;New York, New York;parents;upholstery
point.subjects: ";"Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front;Community and Identity

point.title: History of education
point.keywords: education;Evander Childs High School;Highbridge, New York;Hunter College;junior high school;Lehman College;PS 166;PS 73;PS 93
point.subjects: ";"Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front;Community and Identity

point.title: Discussing her father's pacifist beliefs / Great Depression's effect on father's business and housing situation
point.keywords: family business;family vacation;father;Great Depression;housing;Long Beach, New York;pacifists;Rockaway Beach, New York;socialist
point.subjects: ";"Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front;Community and Identity

point.title: Leaving class to visit a peace rally / Joining the American Student Union
point.subjects: ";"Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front;Community and Identity

point.title: Recounting an experience with an ill behaved teacher
point.subjects: ";"Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front;Community and Identity

point.title: Describing the peace activities she participated in
point.keywords: fascism;Madison Square Park;peace activities;Peace Day;peace rallies;pre-war;Rabbi Wise;student council work
point.subjects: ";"Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front;Community and Identity

point.title: Deciding to fight against fascism
point.keywords: defense training school;Todd shipyards;World War II
point.subjects: ";"Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front;Community and Identity

point.title: Describing the first day working at Todd Shipyards
point.keywords: Erie Basin, Brooklyn;machine shop;Red Hook, Brooklyn;repair yard;shipyard workers;women workers;work commute;work uniform
point.subjects: ";"Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front;Community and Identity

point.title: Describing the training of the other women at the shipyard / Describing wages for women
point.subjects: ";"Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front;Community and Identity

point.title: Describing how men were convinced to allow women in the union
point.subjects: ";"Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front;Community and Identity

point.title: Describing the warning about getting protection / Speaking with management
point.subjects: ";"Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front;Community and Identity

point.title: Minorities working at the shipyard / Treatment of women at the shipyard
point.subjects: ";"Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front;Community and Identity

point.title: Getting fired by management
point.subjects: ";"Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front;Community and Identity

point.title: Meeting with Frances Perkins / End of the war
point.subjects: ";"Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front;Community and Identity

point.title: Thoughts on FDR / Thoughts of others about the war
point.subjects: ";"Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front;Community and Identity

point.title: Conclusion
point.subjects: ";"Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front;Community and Identity

record.description: Justine Eisenberg describes her experience growing up in New York in the 1930s, the toll of the Great Depression on her father's business, and her experience working at the Todd Shipyards during World War II. Eisenberg discusses in great detail the efforts she made to help get women into the shipyard workers Union as part of her fight for equal work and equal pay.
record.transcript:
FARRELL: This is Shanna Farrell with Justine Eisenberg on September 5, 2015. We are in San Francisco, California, and this is session one of our interviews for Rosie the Riveter project. Justine, can you start by telling me where and when you were born and a little bit about your early life?
EISENBERG: Okay. Well, I was born on September 21, 1917 in New York City, in Manhattan. I would say, in general terms, I had a very happy childhood. Grew up in Manhattan—well, somewhere around Columbus Avenue. Apparently at that time people moved rather frequently compared with today, so that I would say up till the war, I moved four times, or my family did, all around the West Side of Manhattan. Oh, and yes, once we moved to the Bronx; that’s the fifth time, that’s right.
FARRELL: Can you tell me your parents’ names and a little bit about each of them, or some of your early memories of them?
EISENBERG: Yes. Both my mother and father were born in what was then called Austria-Hungary. After World War I and up to today, it’s been called Poland. They were born in the big city of Krakow. My mother came from a big family in Krakow, and she apparently had relatives in London, and so more or less when she was a teenager, I don’t know when, she moved to London and worked there for a while. Then she came here, to New York, and lived with her brother for a while. My father was also born in Krakow. Apparently he was an only son, from what I know. He lived in Krakow, and I guess when he was a teenager also, decided to leave because men his age were drafted into the army, and he didn’t want to be part of that. So he moved, really as a teenager, to Germany, where he became an apprentice, worked at an upholstery training outfit, and then decided to come to New York, and I guess landed on the Lower East Side, as most immigrants at that time did. Apparently he had very good training there in Germany and was able to start a business here in New York, in Manhattan. As a matter of fact, on Columbus Avenue, started an upholstery shop there, and that’s the kind of work he did from then on, and apparently was a very good upholsterer and had wonderful clients, for instance, the Explorers Club and people like that. But that was the area, the West Side, that’s the kind of people he would deal with. To grow up a block from Central Park was a wonderful place to grow up.
FARRELL: What were both of your parents’ names?
EISENBERG: My father was Harry Seeman and my mother was Millie Wachtel before she married.
FARRELL: Do you remember when they came over to the States?
EISENBERG: Not exactly; it was somewhere between 1910, 1912, 1914.
FARRELL: Okay. And when they moved here, were you born on the Lower East Side?
EISENBERG: No, I wasn’t. I never lived on the Lower East Side. I was born in the area where I lived, and that was 182 West 89th Street.
FARRELL: Do you remember, was it a Polish community that they were living in?
EISENBERG: Was it what? Polish? No, not at all. I would say, if anything, on Central Park West you had Jewish people. On Columbus Avenue, Amsterdam Avenue, you had Irish people. Broadway was a mix, and West End Avenue, Riverside Drive was Jewish people. But not completely, obviously.
FARRELL: Do you have any siblings?
EISENBERG: Yes. I had a sister who was two and a half years younger than I, and my brother, who was born much later.
FARRELL: Can you tell me a little bit about some of the traditions or some of the celebrations that you had in your family when you were growing up?
EISENBERG: Well, we always celebrated holidays, Jewish holidays, American—well, mainly Jewish holidays and the general American holidays.
FARRELL: Can you tell me a little bit about your education and what you did, maybe some of the subjects in school that you were interested in?
EISENBERG: Well, I went to school first at PS 166, which was on 89th Street between Columbus and Amsterdam, and it was a very fine school, a progressive school. I remember them explaining to us why the way we were learning certain things was very advanced, et cetera. That school had both boys and girls. And then I think, what was it after sixth grade probably, we divided, and girls were sent to PS 93, which was nearby. I’ll tell you a funny thing about PS 93. This was a very old building, very, very old, and the bathrooms were out in the yard way behind the school. They weren’t in the school. And these were all girls in the school, and if you needed to go to the bathroom, you had to pick another girl to go with you, you know, safety’s sake. I remember that. Then, by now we come to about 1929, just before the Depression, and we moved to the Bronx. Oh no, no, before that we moved to Broadway, 2465 Broadway. [laughs] It’s funny how these numbers stick in your mind. We thought at the time that it was a fancy building. It had an elevator, one of these iron gate elevators, you know? And we lived there for a while, and then they decided to throw down that building, so we had to move. We then moved to the Bronx. This, again, I guess was the height of good times, good economic times. We moved to a lovely new apartment building; oh, we thought it was fabulous. Sunken living room and all that kind of thing. That was near Yankee Stadium in Manhattan. It was called Highbridge. Highbridge has suddenly become famous again, with that bridge there. And PS 73 was a junior high school, PS 73. I stayed in that school till I graduated and that was fine. Then I went to Evander Childs High School. I went there even though my teachers were recommending that I go to some other school, which was supposed to be—I don’t know what terms to use—for people who did very well in school. But that would’ve meant leaving all my friends, and it was to be an all-girls school, and I saw no reason for going to an all-girls school. So I went with my friends to Evander Childs High School, which was in the Bronx, in the East Bronx, which was nowhere where we lived. We actually lived in the West Bronx, but that’s how it was. So I went there, to Evander Childs High School. And then, after I graduated from there, I went to Hunter College. The whole gamut—because at that time, you started out in a factory building on 32nd Street, because the old Hunter College was being rebuilt. Then we moved to what’s called Lehman College today, it was then Hunter in the Bronx, and I went there. And then I went downtown, 32nd Street. I never went to the new Hunter College building, which they were building at the time. [break in audio] My father was a strong pacifist. Matter of fact, he was a socialist and a pacifist. And he was the kind of person who never let us wear a cape, because that was too militaristic. And when it came to things like Flag Day or Memorial Day, which is on 89th Street and Riverside Drive, where you had the—monument to WWI veterans {and maybe Civil War veterans}what do you call that, 89th Street and Riverside Drive, you have the whole statue and all that stuff, he would never let us go there.
FARRELL: Do you have a sense of why or where that came from?
EISENBERG: Why he was a pacifist I think because when he grew up there in Krakow, and young men were forced into the army, he was opposed to that. He ran away from there, and he had very, very strong feelings. He was always a pacifist, a socialist.
FARRELL: When you were growing up and there were celebrations like Flag Day, would you want to participate in that?
EISENBERG: Well, I did to a degree because everybody else was doing it. So sure, everybody would go out on those days, I remember, if it was a Sunday or something, everybody put on a nice new coat, you know, and marched around. And I wanted to do that.
FARRELL: What were the sorts of things he would say in response to you wanting to do those things?
EISENBERG: It’s too militaristic. We don’t want to be involved with war.
FARRELL: What was your mother’s reaction to that? Do you remember?
EISENBERG: She supported him.
FARRELL: Supported your father, or you wanting to participate?
EISENBERG: Oh, my feelings about participating, it wasn’t a strong feeling, it was I wanted to do what the rest of the kids were doing.
FARRELL: So, during the Depression, do you remember the effect that that had on your father’s business?
EISENBERG: Well, I don’t think there was anybody who was unaffected by it. Yes, all right, it affected his business seriously, because we had moved to the Bronx, to this great apartment, and we could no longer afford it. So we had to move back downtown again, to Columbus Avenue. He wasn’t happy about it, we weren’t happy about it. He happened to own a small tenement on Columbus Avenue, and so we had to move there. That was an interesting move. We lived on 93rd Street, on Columbus Avenue, and the El train [Elevated train] used to run there, so that right in front of our living room windows, the El was running back and forth all the time. I remember, first of all, the noise of El didn’t bother us after a while, we didn’t even hear it, it didn’t matter. We were very lucky in that he was able—you know, there was no such thing as air-conditioning at that time, and he was able in the summertime to take us out to Rockaway Beach or to Long Beach. They would rent a couple of rooms, and we would go out in a cab; we didn’t have a car. The family, at the beginning of the summer, would go out in a cab to Rockaway or Long Beach, and we would spend the summer there, and then come back. I remember there were times when we were still in Manhattan, I guess at the beginning of the summer or the end of the summer, when it was still very hot—it was very hot, very hot—and we lived in this apartment, a railroad flat, and we would go in the kitchen, you had a fire escape, and we would go and sit on the fire escape to cool off. I would say the fact that we were so lucky as to be able to go to the beach in the summertime—my father taught me to swim at a very young age in the ocean, my sister too. My brother hadn’t come around at that point. And it was very good. My father used to come home every day, say about 5:00 pm, and we’d meet him at the train, and maybe go for a swim with him, certainly on the weekend we did that.
FARRELL: Do you remember any of the other families that lived in the tenement building?
EISENBERG: You mean do I remember them today?
FARRELL: Or do you remember interacting with them, maybe who they were and relationships that your family had with the other people living in the building? Or not really?
EISENBERG: I really don’t. I had close friends, and I was a teenager by that time and lived in the Bronx when I was going to junior high, and I had many friends and hung out with them. What else?
FARRELL: So you went to Hunter College first and then Lehman College?
EISENBERG: No, it was Hunter College all the time. It wasn’t Lehman—Hunter wasn’t Lehman at that point. It became Lehman later. It was all Hunter.
FARRELL: Oh, I see, okay. What were you studying at Hunter?
EISENBERG: I was a poli sci [political science] major. And I thought at that time that I was very much interested in journalism. I thought that’s what I would like to do. And therefore—you were told, therefore you have to major in this or that, I didn’t have any choice. So I became a poli sci major and an English minor.
FARRELL: Were there a lot of other women that you went to college with at that point?
EISENBERG: You mean who were in college with me? Sure. Hunter was all women at that time.
FARRELL: Oh, that’s right. It was all women until recently.
EISENBERG: Oh, much later.
FARRELL: That’s right, I forgot about that. I actually have some family friends who went there when it was all women. I totally forgot about that.
EISENBERG: And I didn’t like that idea that it was all women.
FARRELL: Okay, why didn’t you like that?
EISENBERG: I wondered why do we have to be separate? This is not a normal way of living. [laughs] That particular period—maybe you don’t know, but it’s so vivid to me. That particular period, the Depression and the period before the war was a very active time. I mean, you had the Depression going on. When I went to high school, across the street from Evander Childs High School there were all these Hoovervilles. I don’t know if you’ve heard about those.
FARRELL: I do know what they are, but for people who might listen to this in the future, can you explain what that is or what that means?
EISENBERG: Yes. This was the Depression, people had lost their homes, they had no place to go, and there were empty lots all around there, so people would go and pick up—it was mostly men, there were no women, the men were involved. I remember right across the street from Evander Childs High School there was one of these Hoovervilles, and the men had just fashioned some kind of a dwelling from these scraps of wood and metal and so on, and that’s where they lived. And of course you had people, you know, selling apples all over the place. That’s the kind of thing that was going on. And in the high school—well, I will tell you a funny story. I was always what you might term a peacenik, absolutely a peacenik. I was, you know, I was opposed to war, I had been brought up that way, and I would do anything I could to support activities for peace. So I remember one day in Evander Childs High School people were passing out leaflets saying—oh yes, I should say this started around then, when I was in high school, maybe I was a junior, that they were going to have April, some date in April, maybe, say April 17, there would be a peace rally—there were going to be peace rallies all over the country, and there was going to be one at Evander Childs High School. I was very much interested in it and said, Okay, I’m going to go there. And it said, Meet at 11:00 am at such-and-such a corner. Okay. I didn’t know anybody who was involved with it. I loved social studies, and I was in a social studies class, I walked out of the class. Imagine walking out of a high school and nobody stops you or anything, and walked out of the class to go to the peace meeting. I come to the corner, there’s nobody there. I didn’t know what was going on, and there was nothing else to do, so I just turned around and went back into class. But then, as I said, I was always interested in peace activities. When I graduated from Evander Childs, people in the speech department selected who would be the student representative, who would represent the class at graduation. Evander Childs was one of these very, very maybe 5,000 students large schools. So they asked—I had written a speech for a class session, called Pro-Pacifica, something like that. I wrote this speech, and the speech department loved my speech, so I was selected to be the speaker at the Evander Childs High School graduation. But I was always interested. Then when I got to college, there was just a ferment of activity at that time, at that period, and everybody was always handing out leaflets of all kinds. I would take these leaflets and come home and study them, about this and this and this and that. Many of them were various aspects of socialism, of political people, and they were all bothering me and trying to convince me to join this one or that one. I finally said to several of them, well, you all say these things, you all practically say the same thing in different ways. Why don’t you get together and become a force of some sort? Okay, I come back from the summer vacation, and several of them came to me and said, Okay, Justine, we’ve gotten together. We have an organization. This is the American Student Union. Will you join us? And once I became assured that that’s what it was, I joined the American Student Union, and I became very active there.
FARRELL: Backing up a little bit. So, aside from your upbringing, with your father being a pacifist, what motivated you to get involved with some of these peace activities?
EISENBERG: Well, because I guess I grew up very, you know, under his guidance, I would say. I was always opposed to war. Just as simple as that.
FARRELL: So were there any things that—any points that you were trying to make, or any—I guess I’m sort of interested in what the sticking points were for you about these activities and what brought you to them.
EISENBERG: Well, all right, let me give you—I was, in general, always interested in what was going on in the world. We always had the radio on, we always had discussions. So, all right, I’ll be very specific. But I’ll tell you the story that I was relating. I went to Hebrew school, a very good Hebrew school, when we lived downtown. Then we moved to the Bronx, and my brother was born at that point, so my mother was very busy with him. I was the oldest child in the family, so I had to go and do things, you know, help my mother and take care of things. Anyway, so she sent me to a Hebrew school, which was of a completely different nature than the one I had gone to downtown, and it was all boys, practically, because girls weren’t given that kind of—generally speaking, weren’t given that kind of an education. And I had been at what was called the SAJ [a Jewish school] downtown for about three years, perhaps, maybe even four, and I was well advanced, I could speak Hebrew very well, speak it very well. I’m citing speak it, because that’s what they were encouraging us to do, thinking that people would move to Israel at that time, and therefore we would know how to speak Hebrew. So I was in this Hebrew class, which was composed of all boys, and the teacher comes around and he’s asking everybody about the assignment and to read something or other. And he came around saying—and the boys didn’t know, they couldn’t read the language. And he was very angry, and he was going around with a ruler, and he says, “If you can’t do the assignment, I’m going to hit you on the head with a ruler.” And I’m looking at this totally astonished. What kind of a way is that for a teacher to behave? So he comes around to the boys, one after another, and then he comes to me, and I was completely aghast. He comes there and I said, “You dare touch me with that ruler, and I’ll throw this book at you,” which to me, that was—so he looks at me, how dare anybody, let alone a girl, throw a book at somebody? So he starts to throw the book at me, or no, the ruler, so I took the book and I just threw it at him. And I said to the boys, “What’s the matter with you people? Why do you sit here. I’m walking out of this place. You don’t belong in a school like this. I won’t stay in a school like this.” And I just walked out and said, “Come on, boys.” And they just all followed me, of course. Well, whoever heard of such behavior. And I came home and told my mother about it, and she said, “Good for you. That’s no way for a teacher to behave.” So then, a short time afterwards, the teacher comes to complain to my mother about me. My mother said, “She was absolutely right, the way she behaved, and I wouldn’t want her in this school anymore.” So therefore she had to find another school for me, and she couldn’t find anything in that area. She found a place in Washington Heights, so I would walk across the bridge, High Bridge, to Washington Heights and go to school there, and my girlfriends came with me, and we would go there. So that’s an example.
FARRELL: When you were at Hunter, can you tell me about some of the activities that you were involved in, the peace-related activities or demonstration-related activities that you were involved in?
EISENBERG: What other activities?
FARRELL: The demonstrations or the peace activities.
EISENBERG: Well, peace, I was always involved in them, and there were activities all the time.
FARRELL: Can you tell me about some of the activities?
EISENBERG: Yes. Well, there was always, around the middle of April, all over the country, as a matter of fact in England and, I think, around the world, there was maybe Peace Day or something like that, and there were always rallies and meetings. Then I was in Hunter downtown at 32nd Street, and we would get together with the people in City College on 23rd Street. We didn’t call it—what’s it called today? Whatever. Anyway, we called it City 23rd street. City 23rd street and Hunter would get together and have a big peace rally at Madison Square Park all of that time. Then there was another person who, I guess he was my guru. You may have never heard of him, a man named Rabbi Stephen Wise. He was very well known at the time, really very well. I guess he was the president of this organization. And he was a customer of my father’s. The story is, it’s a little mixed up because it doesn’t make sense, in a way. My mother and father are in the park pushing me in a baby carriage, and Rabbi Wise comes and meets my father, “Hello.” “Hello.” “Baby.” “Baby.” “What is her name?” or “What are you going to call her?” I say it’s a little mixed up, because here I am being pushed in a carriage, I must have had a name by that time. He says, “What are you going to call her?” And my father said, “Well, we’re thinking of Peace or Justice or Liberty.” And Rabbi Wise said to my father, “You can’t do that to a girl.” And he said, “Why don’t you call her Justine.” So that’s how I came to my name. And I always felt I’ve got to live up to my name. I really feel that way, and that’s who I am.
FARRELL: Okay, so you were involved—when you were at Hunter, you were involved in the American Student Union and you participated—
EISENBERG: And student council. I was a member of student council. I was very active.
FARRELL: What were some of the activities that you did in terms of you activism as a part of the student union and the student council?
EISENBERG: Honestly, I can’t remember anything in particular. It was all the general activities of the times I was reacting to. I was always involved with peace work in the American Student Union, that was a big thing. I remember first when I became a member of student council, there were questions about the prices in the lunchroom, that the lunchroom was too expensive. So student council, and I was part of it, set up a committee to lower the prices in the lunchroom. I mean, this kind of thing. Whatever there were, there were issues of the time. I don’t particularly remember any of them right now.
FARRELL: So when the war started—
EISENBERG: They were all mainly, there were a lot of peace activities, because this was what was taking place. War was coming. But at that point, I was very much involved in the peace part of it, and let’s not become involved with war.
FARRELL: Did you have a sense that war was coming?
EISENBERG: After a while, yes, it was very clear, yes.
FARRELL: How was it clear? Can you give me an example of how you knew?
EISENBERG: Well, all the events of the time. Well, and look at fascism. I mean, that was a big point. And you had FDR—well, first of all, there was fascism, there was Hitler, and we were all opposed to that. Of course the ASU was completely involved with that kind of thing. I don’t know what else to say, it was so obvious.
FARRELL: So World War II started, and you were at Hunter, but you left Hunter to—
EISENBERG: No, let’s come before that. So, it became clear that war was coming and that we were going to war. And here was I, who had always been active against war, suddenly coming to the conclusion that fascism is a very serious problem, it’s affecting us, look at what’s happening in Europe. Even though we didn’t know the whole story, look at what’s happening to the Jewish people there. It was very obvious, so that gradually I came to the conclusion, no, we’ve got to defeat fascism. And this became the most important thing. So I became very much involved in that, and at some point I decided I would like to do something about this personally. I couldn’t see myself joining, what did they call it, the army or anything like that, the navy. That was, you know, nowhere in my background. But that I wanted to play a very active role in fighting fascism and doing something about the war. So I thought about it, and said, I think—at that point, my love for school, I forgot about it. It didn’t seem to be relevant to me at the time. It was much more important for me to do something about peace, about getting peace. Or defeating fascism, let me put it that way. So I decided I was going to leave Hunter for a period of time and get some defense training so that if there were a possibility that I would get an active job in terms of the war. So I decided to go to defense training school. Another friend of mine and I, she was going to City College, I was going to Hunter, and we both decided we would do this and join a defense training school, which were starting all over the place. There was a defense training school in Bayonne, and we would go to Bayonne from Manhattan to go to defense training school, where we learned the basics of machine shop work, and we certainly spent a lot of time learning how to read blueprints, run a drill press, all this kind of thing, and we were doing that. And then suddenly, one day in the New York Times, this could even have been October of 1941, an article appears on the front page of the New York Times that Todd Shipyards is going to hire women to work in the shipyard. So I said, Wow, that’s what I want to do. My friend wasn’t interested in that, she was having physical problems as a result of doing work in a machine shop, so she couldn’t do that. I said, No, I’m going to go apply for a job at Todd Shipyards. Which I did. I guess you had to write a letter or something, or call up for an application, whatever it was, I did that, and said this is what I would like to do. And very quickly, I got a response, come for an interview.
FARRELL: Backing up a little bit, do you remember who was running the defense training schools?
EISENBERG: The government, the federal government.
FARRELL: And how long were the—
EISENBERG: The courses were probably about six weeks or something. Something like that. Because shops were springing up all over the place. But, you know, they weren’t particularly exciting, but I guess I had just about finished that course when this article appeared, and so I wrote and was called for an interview. I didn’t know what this meant, but it sounded very exciting to me.
FARRELL: Where were the Todd Shipyards?
EISENBERG: Todd Shipyards were in Erie Basin in Brooklyn, which was Red Hook. I had to take the subway to go to Smith and 9th Street, which was a territory I knew nothing whatever about. So that’s what I did, I went for an interview, and they listened to the fact that I had some background, and they said, Very good, so I was hired for the machine shop. And then, I remember—well, I can tell you many parts of that. I mean, now we’re talking about something that was really exciting. How shall I start? Well, I’ll start with the first day I went to work there. So what I had to do, I was then living on 95th Street between Columbus and the park, had to go to the subway on 96th Street and Central Park West. You had to be in the shipyard at 7:00 in the morning prepared to work, with your clothing changed and all that kind of thing. So I had to leave my home at about 5:30 in the morning to be there at 7:00 in the morning. I go walking down 95th Street to Central Park and 96th Street, take the subway to this foreign territory, Smith and 9th Street, and walking through Red Hook in the dark. And there was this housing project, this big housing project there, Columbia Street, I remember. I forget the—
FARRELL: It’s still there.
EISENBERG: And it was a little scary, you know, really scary. But okay, we get there. Then, first thing we had to do, Todd Shipyards, everything was cost-plus. And when they hired women, they had to build a whole new building for the women, and they built a beautiful dressing room for us. And all—no, let me put it this way. Women were hired for all the various departments, for the machine shop, welding, burning, ship fitting, everything else, many different departments. The first days, the first week or so, there were only twelve women hired for the machine shop. The person who was the head guy for all of the men, he trained all the men, and had been doing this for many, many years, so every machinist there had been trained by this particular guy. He was very good, and we were fortunate enough to be trained by him as well. We come in there—well, I’ll give it to you as it happened—and you looked at this wonderful dressing area, with this big, it was like a, what can I say, a big circular place where you would wash yourself. You could take off your clothes and wash yourself in there. How were we to come dressed? Well, we had been told. We had helmets—you had to change into helmets and these heavy shoes, and it was going to be cold, heavy underwear, everything heavy, heavy, heavy clothing. Then you could change in that dressing area and wash up later. Okay, so my most vivid memory, it probably was wintertime, all the girls, all the women are running around with long red underwear. Everybody had red underwear and everybody’s running around in their red underwear. That gives you a picture of things there. But all right, let me come back to the first day. Well, you can imagine, there were at least 10,000, at some point there were 20,000 men working in the yard. So my particular point of view of these twelve women coming in to the—well, you can imagine, all these guys coming to look at the girls coming to work, of course. We had to walk through a whole gauntlet of everybody looking and whistling and all this kind of thing. But it was fun. I, who under ordinary circumstances, would not approve of this kind of behavior, I just looked at it as part of the fun of doing something like this. Come into the yard, and it’s an old, old shipyard, concrete floors, and on top of the concrete floors were like wooden boards, a platform on which we had to work. I was assigned to work at a lathe, a 16-inch lathe, which is a very large lathe. There’s the machine itself standing there, and then you had the belts that you had to have like a long stick or something, that in order to change the speed, you had to change the—they were things that measured or described how you worked on this lathe. I had to stand on this platform and push over the belt in order to get the machine going. Okay, they showed us how to do that, we did. And then the head of the machine shop would let us run the drill press or other things. Now, the important thing about this is that this was a repair yard. We were not manufacturing new pieces of equipment, which meant we of course had to know how to read a blueprint and what we were doing, and we had to have some understanding of what was involved in that. And that made it very interesting, because you had—which was different from what was, my opinion was that on the West Coast they weren’t doing, the women were not doing this kind of thing. They were mostly working on new ships. So there was a total difference between a repair yard and where they were building new ships. But it became very interesting working on all these different pieces of machinery.
FARRELL: Before you started working on various pieces, you had gone to defense training school, and then when you got the job, what was the training like that you received on the job?
EISENBERG: Well, this was part of it, you know. They showed us how to handle the lathe, he saw that I knew how to run a drill press, he saw that I knew how to read a blueprint, they were checking on that. And then they were training us to know when you got a particular machine part replicate, this particular piece of machinery, to understand how to do this.
FARRELL: How long did that process take?
EISENBERG: Well, it became part of the daily work that we did.
FARRELL: Do you remember a length of time where he kind of saw, “Oh okay, she understands how to do X and Y and Z,” and then when you were actually starting to do the work?
EISENBERG: It just happened automatically, yeah. There was no particular period of time.
FARRELL: Do you remember, did the other women there have similar training as you, or did they have to receive training?
EISENBERG: In the machine shop? I think some of them had to have more training than I did because they hadn’t had it before. Interestingly enough—I’m going to deal with different things—the women in the machine shop, I was not the only college person there. There were other people who were college women. There were people with all kinds of backgrounds. It wasn’t as you might have thought in an ordinary machine shop, where new people are coming in, it was completely different, because people did have two things. They had a fairly extensive educational background, and they also had some kind of experience in machine shop work, yes. But he was always there to help us and work with us, the foreman. I’ll also say that after a while—to me, this is important. A number of the men spoke to me, and they saw that I was standing on these pieces of wood because of the concrete ground. And they said, You know, you better be very careful—they thought I shouldn’t stand on this stuff—because you’re going to hurt your legs. Your legs, later on, it’s not going to have a good effect on your legs. And I’ve wondered at this point, do some of my problems go back to that? I don’t know. I don’t think I worked there long enough for it to have affected me very much. But it was just an interesting thought. So all the women in my machine shop were doing this kind of thing. It was very cold there, because it was right on the harbor, the doors were open if there were any doors, the wind was blowing, there’s this concrete floor. It was very, very cold, and you had to be dressed very warmly. Okay, now the other part of it was that when we were hired, we were told there is a union here and we have a union contract, and you will start, I believe, I’m not sure, I could check it, looking back some of my writing, I think that we started at something like, as helpers, we did start as helpers, and we started at 87 cents an hour. Don’t quote me on that, but that’s what I recall. And we were told there was a wage scale, which went up to level 1, level 2, and level 3. Level 3 was $1.20 an hour. I guess level 2 was $1.12. And what happened, I don’t remember the interval between the 87 cents and this, but that was the level at which the women started. And they said, You are hired for 87 cents an hour as a helper, and that’s your classification, as a helper. That’s that. And you’re not going to go any further, that’s the level at which you’re going to stay. Okay, so all the girls, of course, were very excited about everything and we talked about it. And since we were all together, women from all the departments were all together in this one big dressing room, we all talked together. We started talking about the wages, and we said, Well, if there’s a union here, and if the men can go up the wage scale, what’s the matter with us? Why shouldn’t we go up the way that they do? And we started talking about it together and said, You know, why don’t we talk and see if we can’t be treated the same way the men are? Why should there be a difference?
FARRELL: One quick question. So there was a union already in place.
EISENBERG: Oh yes, it had been in place for many years.
FARRELL: And it was just men who were able to join at that point?
EISENBERG: Yes, there was no question, because they had been working there all this time.
FARRELL: And was there a difference in the wages that you were making between the men—men and women were making different amounts of money?
EISENBERG: Well, that’s the point I’m making. That’s the very point I’m making. We were hired just to stay at one level. They were going up $.87, blah blah, to $1.12, to $1.20. And it was the union that had, with management, established this wage level. And we weren’t part of it. [break in audio] —The union was named the Industrial Union and Marine and Shipbuilding Workers of America, and this was for Todd Shipyards, which is where we worked, and I knew that there was—a friend of mine who worked in Hoboken at Todd Shipyards, and I knew of other people, I didn’t know them but I knew the woman in Hoboken, there were other women working at Todd Shipyards in Camden. And these, of course, were all organized by the same union, and we were Local 39 in the union. So the women got together and said, This is fine, and we’re earning good money—and it was good money for those days—but why should we be treated differently than the men? That’s very clear. So we got together and talked about it, and we realized, Well, we need to talk to the union and see what they could do for us. We should become members of the union. We didn’t know whether they would admit us or not. And we all, in different departments, began talking to the guys, and they all said—I shouldn’t talk in such generalities. You know, they’re not going to let you join the union, or why should you get the same pay we do? You’re not going to stay here after the war. We’re going to be here. Why should you have to come into the union? And so on. So we tried to talk to them and explain to them, we did. We said, Look, if they’re going to keep us at this wage, then what’s going to happen when you go and negotiate with management? They’re going to say, We don’t need to raise your wages if we can get the women—here we have all these people who will work for 87 cents an hour. So we convinced—finally, we convinced many of the men it was in their interest to let us into the union, to join the union.
FARRELL: How did you convince them that it was in their interest?
EISENBERG: Well, first we talked to them individually, and we were able to convince some of them. Others were opposed under any circumstances, because they didn’t think women belonged in the shipyard, they’d go back to cooking after the war.
FARRELL: But what were the things that you were trying to convince them of, that it was in their best interest? How was it in their best interest? What would you tell them?
EISENBERG: We would tell them, if they can hire women for 87 cents an hour, why can’t they hire more women and take your jobs from you? It was obvious to us. And we began to convince many of them of that. So what do we have to do? We asked the guys who are in the union, who do we have to talk to at the union? And we found out that there was an executive board, which met at such-and-such a place. I remember there was a guy—I finally found out there was one particular guy who was German, he had a background in unions and so on, and I thought, well, he would understand this. So we asked for a meeting with the executive board, and a few of us were selected to go and talk to the executive board, and we did. At first they laughed and said the same thing, you’re all going to go back to the kitchen afterwards, you know, blah blah blah. And we said, Okay, we think this is to your advantage to have us in the union, with the same argument, et cetera. And we finally convinced this Dick Virsch, who was the key guy I was thinking of, and convinced another guy who was the secretary of the union, and they say, Well, all right, we’ll have to talk to the executive board about it. So we asked to speak before a meeting of the executive board, and we kept going through this same argument and the same education of the men. We did, and we were successful. Then they said, Well, all right, you’re going to have to get the membership to agree to this. Okay. So meanwhile, the girls are in every shop and they are able to talk to the guys everywhere. So there comes a time for the general union meeting, we come to the union meeting, and the same discussion goes on all the time, and we get the majority to agree with us. Okay. But I have to explain to you. It sounds so simple; it wasn’t that simple at all. And in addition to that, you need to know that at that time there were people there, men there, who were opposed to the war, who really were isolationists, who really were opposed to the war. And we were warned, be careful for such-and-such and such-and-such, et cetera. And there were people who said to me, Justine, you have to have protection, because you know what happens with people when this gets tough. These guys are tough guys, and there have been people who found themselves, when they were opposed to whatever the issue was, who just found themselves in the drink there. You’ve really got to get protection. So I would have people always on the lookout for me as I walked through the yard.
FARRELL: “In the drink” isn’t a term I’m familiar with. Can you explain to me what that means?
EISENBERG: To just be thrown into the water.
FARRELL: And when they suggested that you have protection, what kind of protection were they talking about? How did they see that working?
EISENBERG: They were just watching out for me, they were behind me, et cetera. So when I speak about coming to a meeting, these other guys are at the meeting too. So there would be this whole discussion going on constantly, but in every shop the same kind of discussion. The fact that the women were together there in that one place, we could talk together every day. The women were 100 percent in favor of this. The women came to every meeting. There were some women, I guess, who had children, young children, and couldn’t leave them. I also need to explain that, as I said, some of the, or a large number—no, a strong number of the women who were working there in Todd Shipyard, at least where I was working, were people who had gone to college. It wasn’t just people who had come for a job because they needed a job and that was a job where they could earn good money. There was strong feeling about the war. So that’s what happened. And then finally they said, All right, we’ve got to go to management. We go to management, and I remember being called to the executive board meeting, the executive board would decide who was going to talk to management about it. And the men on the executive board said, You know, we don’t know. We can’t present the argument. We really—we’ve never dealt with that kind of an argument before, and we need some help in presenting the argument to management as to why women should be pain the same rate—the argument was “equal pay for equal work.” We need help. So we discussed it, and it was decided that I would go and speak to management. Oh, no, I guess we—I spoke to management, and management said no. And the contract said if there was a difference of opinion, you had to go to arbitration. So that’s when I was asked, because I went with other women to management, but at arbitration there could only be one person. So I was asked to go and present the argument to the arbitrator The arbitrator, I’ve completely forgotten the name, has name. Very famous judge, very well-known judge at that time. I remember he was always quoted in the Times on all kinds of issues. So I go there and present the argument to the arbitrator, who listened, asked me all kinds of questions, and then at the end, I have to say, he complimented me on my presentation, thought I did an excellent job, and the decision was “Yes, women can come in, can join the union, and will be paid equal pay for equal work.”
FARRELL: How did you prepare for the presentation?
EISENBERG: Well, I met with the other women, I met with the men, I spoke to people I knew, other people who were involved in unions, although it didn’t help that much, really. Because our situation was different. This was an existing union, an existing shop for many, many years. [audio break] 1942.
FARRELL: And then at this period, were there more women joining? Did more women besides those original twelve keep joining?
EISENBERG: Oh yes, yes. People were being hired all the time. I would say at that point there were probably about 20,000 men in the shipyard, and not a tremendously large number of women. I honestly don’t remember. Maybe three were 200 or 300 women in the shipyard.
FARRELL: What was the time period of them joining? I mean, those 300 women, was it a quick pace that they were joining? New people every day, or once a week?
EISENBERG: Well, this would be, once the decision was made, everybody who was there joined almost right away. Of course the women were—there would be union meetings once a month, and the women, because they were so much interested in this, came in large numbers to the union meeting, and they would really speak up.
FARRELL: But leading up to the women being allowed in the union and the equal pay for equal work, leading up to that, do you remember the pace with which women came to work in the shipyard, in Todd Shipyards?
EISENBERG: Well, look, the whole thing was just a short period of time. It was about four years, the whole war situation.
FARRELL: When you started, you mentioned there were just twelve, but by the time you were able to unionize, there were about 300. Were women continuously joining? Yes, many people, including women, were hired at a rapid pace. We were in the middle of a war and many workers were needed.
EISENBERG: Once people heard about it, the women joined right away.
FARRELL: Okay. And did you have to convince them? Because you said everybody was in 100 percent agreement.
EISENBERG: Once we talked about it and we talked about equal pay for equal work, of course the women said yes. They were 100 percent for it.
FARRELL: What about other minorities? Were there African Americans that were working in the shipyard?
EISENBERG: There were a number of African-Americans. Not a large number, but there were African-Americans. There were Irish women, Italian women, you know, all—I don’t remember any predominance of any particular—
FARRELL: Were maybe minority men, were they involved in the union? Were they allowed to join?
EISENBERG: Everybody, everybody who worked there was encouraged to join the union.
FARRELL: But before you were able to unionize, do you remember them being in the union, or did they—I guess what I’m asking is±
EISENBERG: Most of the men were in the union.
FARRELL: What I’m trying to get at a little bit is, before you joined the union and women were able to join, were other men who were minorities, were they allowed to join, or did they support your—
EISENBERG: Yes.
FARRELL: Okay. I didn’t know if they weren’t allowed to join, if they were also involved with your efforts, because they also wanted equal pay for equal work.
EISENBERG: There was no such thing that they weren’t allowed to join the union. [audio break] —is that there were a number of black women who, you know, were completely in solidarity with us. And there were black men who worked there. That was no issue. I mean, to my general knowledge at that point, I don’t remember that being any kind of an issue. It was not like today, it’s a completely different situation.
FARRELL: Was there any—do you remember, were you treated like an equal—aside from the union side, when you started working there, were you treated like an equal?
EISENBERG: I would say—you mean as a worker in training?
FARRELL: Yes, by the men.
EISENBERG: By the men? Well, it was a combination of things. By most of the men, not all of them. I indicated that there were men who were opposed to it. They just liked talking and kibitzing with the women and becoming friendly, so we were treated quite well, I think. There weren’t people—they didn’t put us down, really.
FARRELL: And this in terms of—
EISENBERG: And they liked, the guys liked to be working with the women. Of course.
FARRELL: And so in terms of—was your platform for unionizing equal pay for equal work?
EISENBERG: Equal pay for equal work.
FARRELL: Or did it have anything to do with workplace safety? Because I know sort of how the labor movement has worked, is part of it is pay, but part of it is also workplace safety. Did that play a role?
EISENBERG: The main thing with us was equal pay for equal work. It was really very, very strong. And honestly, I would say I don’t think we were there long enough to get to know too much about workplace safety.
FARRELL: Can you tell me about after you were able to successfully unionize—well, I guess in that process, can you tell me about maybe some of the challenges that you faced during that whole period of trying to get equal pay for equal work? Some of the challenges that you faced in unionizing women.
EISENBERG: Well, the main challenge was to convince all the different people there. That was the main challenge. [audio break] —I mentioned with management, they were opposed to the idea of equal pay for equal work, and that was the issue. That was the issue.
FARRELL: So after you were able to—can you tell me about what happened after you were able to unionize?
EISENBERG: So all the women—the women practically 100 percent joined the union. The guys loved that idea, and they would love to come to union meetings because the women would all be there. Really, it was a big social thing as well. I really can’t—
FARRELL: So you met at one point with the secretary of labor, Frances Perkins.
EISENBERG: No, let me go a little bit further. We decided—I remember one of the things that we did, that we should have like a newsletter, the union should have a newsletter, and it hadn’t had anything like that. So many of the women, you knew a little bit about writing, and so we developed a union newspaper, or newsletter, whatever it was called. We put that out to advise people of everything. Now, you asked me before what else did the women do? I remember one fun thing that we did, we were working many long hours at that time, and we wanted to have a little relaxation. There was, what’s the name of the hotel in Brooklyn, downtown Brooklyn? St. Helen or something like that. We decided to go set up a women’s swim club, so we did that, and that was fun, brought the women together. This went on for a while, and I was very busy, both with running my lathe and loving that work, but loving my union work. I really loved it, and I then spent a lot of time on that. And of course management didn’t like that idea. And after a while, they said they were going to fire me because I was missing too much time. Now, ordinarily people are allowed to do union work as part of their job, and they felt I was doing a little too much of that. So they decided to fire me. So the union said, All right, we’ve got to take that back to arbitration, and at that point the—you know, I was young [laughs], and the arbitrator said, “Look, you’re a very smart lady, but you should know that you shouldn’t have been spending that much time, it was not right.” And so they agreed to fire me, and they did. And when I came back and the men heard about it, everywhere, it just went through the yard: Strike, strike, strike. We have to strike.
FARRELL: In response to your firing they wanted to strike?
EISENBERG: Yes, yes. Yes, they didn’t want me to go. So they said strike, and I said, It’s the war. We’re here to help the war. We can’t strike at this point. And they were really outraged. Now, lunchtime there, people used to gather in the yard and have lunch, and right outside everybody would be there, and there were big mobs of people saying, Strike, strike, and I just had to get up and say, You can’t strike. This is unfair and it’s not right. But we’re at war, and that’s the important thing. So we can’t. And I said, Please, just stop. I don’t want any part of this. And they agreed, or most of them did. So I left there, and the union, there was no problem. They got me a job at another shop in another shipyard in Port Newark, which was not Todd Shipyard, but it was in New Jersey.
EISENBERG: It’s in the middle when you go from New York through the Holland Tunnel and the Pulaski Skyway—
FARRELL: In New Jersey.
EISENBERG: And there was Kearny Shipyard and Port Newark Shipyard there. So I got another job there, again working on a lathe. It was a different kind of lathe, and this was a new shop, because it was part of the war effort, again—Port Newark was part of New York Ship, and then this new building was built in Port Newark. So the women worked in Port Newark, which was between Jersey City and Newark. That was a larger yard altogether, and there were more women working there than there had been in Brooklyn. So I got a job there as a lathe operator, and I became active in the union there too, because I began to love this kind of activity. There was a union election, and I was elected to be on the grievance committee.
FARRELL: What was the union? What was this union?
EISENBERG: This was the same union, and this was Local 16. So I was elected to be on the grievance committee. So we dealt with different grievances. There were four or five of us on the grievance committee, and we would go around dealing with the grievances, which was very interesting. And then, because there was even larger number of women there, somehow I became involved with—women in various unions, I guess it was Frances Perkins who called together women from different unions, you know, all kinds of unions on the East Coast, and she gathered women, and because she knew of me through the union, I was invited to come to this meeting where she wanted to talk with us about how we’ve got to plan for the post-war period. And it was fascinating, really fascinating. How are we going to keep women in industry during the post-war period. We talked about that, and talked about what are the demands we would make, what would women need to have, and I remember the first argument and the first requirement would be arranging for day care for children. It was striking that that was the main thing. If women are going to stay in industry, they’re going to need to have day care for children, so we have to plan for that. And a whole plan was worked out together with Frances Perkins.
FARRELL: Do you remember some of the other demands that you wanted or things that you were trying to—how you would get women to stay, some of the other points?
EISENBERG: Well, we were still talking, of course, about equal pay for equal work, because you always had to talk about that. Really the main thing was this business about child care. And there was some talk at that time about safety issues.
FARRELL: So, two questions. Three questions. What year did you leave the shipyard in Erie Basin, in Red Hook, and start at Local 16 in New Jersey?
EISENBERG: I’m trying to think, because it’s all together in my mind. I would say it was probably about two, two and a half years at Todd, the rest was in Port Newark.
FARRELL: Like maybe 1944 and 1945?
EISENBERG: Yes.
FARRELL: Do you remember what year you met with Frances Perkins?
EISENBERG: Yes, it was probably 1944.
FARRELL: So safety issues started to come up. Can you tell me a little bit more about that?
EISENBERG: I don’t remember, because it involved all the other unions, and I don’t particularly remember that.
FARRELL: So your main focus the whole time was still equal pay for equal work, and then for the future it was child care.
FARRELL: When you moved to Local 16, was it difficult for you to get involved in the union, or were you just able to join right away?
EISENBERG: Like that. There was no problem about joining the union, so far as I’m concerned there was no problem joining the union. Once the agreement had come, that was it.
FARRELL: Do you remember—
EISENBERG: I would say there were more black people involved there, because again, it was a larger place. And there were a larger number of women there. And there were people of all backgrounds.
FARRELL: When you met with Frances Perkins and the other women from other unions, do you remember—can you tell me a little bit more about the other women that you met at that point who were involved in unionizing?
EISENBERG: Oh, I remember there were women from all the different industries.
FARRELL: Which industries?
EISENBERG: Well, of course there were the needle trades, you know, obviously in Paterson and Passaic woolen mills, and Botany people were there, electrical workers, a lot of electrical workers. I don’t know, I can’t think of others. [break in audio]
EISENBERG: —had contact with all these unions, so what?
JOANIE EISENBERG: Steel workers? [daughter of Justine]
EISENBERG: There might’ve been steel workers.
JOANIE EISENBERG: The auto industry? People from Detroit, from the auto industry, were they there?
EISENBERG: No, I would say the people who were there were mostly East Coast workers.
FARRELL: What was the outcome of that meeting?
EISENBERG: Well, we drew up a plan, it was a very good plan. Unfortunately, I had the plan, I was writing it, and I gave it to my daughter at some point, who wanted it, and she lost it, so I don’t have it. But it’s gone.
FARRELL: I guess the immediate outcomes after that meeting. I mean, what changed?
EISENBERG: What changed was that the war ended, really. We never got to pass that. Now, one of our ideas was to keep women in the jobs that they had succeeded in getting during the course of the war, to keep women in those industries, like in ship building. And the interesting thing was that, at Local 16 where I was, at New York Ship, my husband worked in Kearny, and he was laid off before I was. Women did work a little longer than men in some departments, it depended upon the situation. But the war had already ended, and we were still—or I was still working there. But then, you know—
FARRELL: Were you planning on staying, working there for an extended period of time after the war?
EISENBERG: Well, I was interested, but—
FARRELL: And did you meet your husband—how did you meet your husband? Was it through your time in the shipyards?
EISENBERG: No, I had known him before then.
FARRELL: Okay.
EISENBERG: Although there were many romances. [laughs]
FARRELL: So I guess being involved with unionizing and these efforts, what did that mean to you?
EISENBERG: It was very exciting. I loved it. You know, I felt—it was, you could undertake doing something that was difficult, but that was necessary, and you could achieve some success. It was a constant struggle, but it was very interesting. The other thing that I loved about it, but, you know, there were exciting Local 16—we met, the union had an office, it was in Journal Square. I would come at the end of the day in Port Newark and go to Journal Square and meet with the other guys there and do various things. But by that time, there was the election coming up, for FDR’s fourth term, the end of the war, 1944, and we were very much involved in that kind of thing. I remember—and it wasn’t an easy struggle to reelect FDR. I remember many times standing in Journal Square making speeches for FDR and so on. It was a big struggle, and I well remember the day that that all ended. I was in the union office, and my god.
FARRELL: When the war ended?
EISENBERG: When FDR died.
FARRELL: Can you tell me a little bit more about the level of patriotism that you experienced with other people in the Todd Shipyards and then in the shipyards in New Jersey?
EISENBERG: Well, as I said before, there were people in Todd Shipyard who were very clearly not in favor of the war, that was true. Certainly it wasn’t a majority opinion, but it was a strong opinion against FDR. There was a lot of feeling against FDR. So far as the war, I remember there were many Italians who supported Mussolini, stuff like that.
FARRELL: I guess sort of part of what I’m wondering is, maybe women or men that you met, were their reason for being there doing those jobs because they were there to support the country? Just the general feeling.
EISENBERG: Well, I think, first of all it was a good job. You got good pay. That was a strong feeling. And I think all the women I spoke with, they were proud of what they were doing for the country, absolutely. Absolutely, yes.
FARRELL: Can you tell me what you remember about when you found out the war was over? Can you tell me about that day, or how you found out?
EISENBERG: I remember, the main thing I remember was when FDR died. That was—but the war was over—well, all I remember was the general joy that the war was over. Of course that was the feeling. I mean, everybody went down to Times Square to celebrate. There’s no question about it.
FARRELL: Can you tell me about the celebration? Did you go to Times Square?
EISENBERG: Yes.
FARRELL: Can you tell me about that celebration in Times Square?
EISENBERG: Oh, it was jam-packed full of excitement and joy. It was wonderful. It was fantastic.
FARRELL: After the war ended, how did you see New York City change? What was different?
EISENBERG: Well, everybody was coming back, all the guys were coming back. Well, for one thing, women did lose jobs, as I said. I was working, still, for a surprising amount of time at the end of the war, and then that ended. As a matter of fact, at that particular moment I had married, and I had moved to Paterson, my husband was working in Paterson, and we moved to Paterson. So I wasn’t in New York as much, I came back shortly after that. But in general, it took time to change. It took time to change. I would say—it’s hard to say, you know, because I’m looking back at it from now to how many women, yeah, well, you know, their husbands came back, they had babies, there were a lot of babies being born. And it took time to change.
FARRELL: What were some of the biggest things that you learned about yourself or in general from that period of time?
EISENBERG: Well, I learned that perhaps I was a good organizer. [laughs] And I enjoyed doing that kind of thing. I enjoyed working with people, but I always enjoyed working with people. I felt I had matured in terms of my knowledge of people and things like that. I got married, that changes things. I don’t know.
FARRELL: No, that’s great. When you talk about your time unionizing and your experiences on the homefront in World War II, what are some of the big things that you want people to know about that period of your life?
EISENBERG: I would like to tell people—I would like people to know the story, the fact that you can achieve something like equal pay for equal work, that it’s possible, that it’s still a hard job, even today. But it’s possible, and you have to keep at it. Well, I think it’s a matter of, look, there are plenty of unions around here, no question about that, where people have to join—where they have to join the union, it’s a closed shop, and they never go to a meeting and they pay dues and they don’t know what it’s all about. But you need a union with leadership that’s fighting for something.
FARRELL: Is that what you see as the importance of unions in general? What, for you, do you find important about unions?
EISENBERG: I think that unions are important in general. Unions can win tremendous and necessary victories for people. But you need to have, in the leadership there, a degree of, what shall I say, determination to carry through what a union is supposed to do, and it doesn’t always happen.
FARRELL: Were you familiar at all with any of the unions in different parts of the country?
EISENBERG: During the war? No.
FARRELL: After the war—
EISENBERG: What I do know is that as a result of what we had done, the people in Hoboken organized the same thing, the people in Camden organized, and then Todd Shipyard had other shipyards up and down the coast, and they joined the same union.
FARRELL: After the war ended and you were starting to learn more about other union efforts during that period of time, what were some of the differences that you saw in union organizing on the East Coast versus other parts of the country, perhaps the West Coast or the Midwest?
EISENBERG: No, I saw that there was a difference between when you had a union like Harry Bridges’s union, you know, the longshore union is a very strong union. To me, that’s as much as I knew about it. I mean, now I heard in speaking with the Rosie the Riveters, you know, how much did it mean. It was something they had to do, but they didn’t see any big significance to it. I didn’t have any sense that they had any sense that it was a struggle to get a union and all these things.
FARRELL: What would your ideal union look like, in a perfect world?
EISENBERG: It would be honest, it would be democratic, it would have clear objectives and a plan for carrying them out.
FARRELL: And so you recently visited the Rosie the Riveter park in Richmond, California. Can you tell me about your experience visiting the park, and maybe what that mean to you, to go see something like that and meet other Rosies?
EISENBERG: Well, I thought it was interesting. I had a sense of what their experience would be like, that it would be very different from our experience. It interested me, just as you indicated, so those members of the union, and they were happy in what they were doing, and there was all the business of patriotism, but I didn’t see much more than that. And I know, you know, that there were unions that can do a very good job. Too few and far between, unfortunately.
FARRELL: Is there anything else that you want to add?
EISENBERG: Well, look, I could make as a general statement. There is a serious economic situation in the country that is affecting many people. It’s very difficult. It would certainly help if people—if unions were involved in this kind of thing more effectively doing something about it, and that people were convinced that they could do something about it. We need that.
FARRELL: Well, thank you very much. I appreciate your time.
EISENBERG: Thank you very much, Shanna.
